{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27489","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27489/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27489/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27489/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27489","id":2559345547,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86YjIOL","number":27489,"title":"BUG: Turning off Intel's 80-bit extended precision causes numpy to not recognize long double type","user":{"login":"ernesto-ren","id":98853673,"node_id":"U_kgDOBeRjKQ","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/98853673?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ernesto-ren","html_url":"https://github.com/ernesto-ren","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ernesto-ren/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ernesto-ren/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ernesto-ren/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ernesto-ren/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ernesto-ren/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ernesto-ren/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ernesto-ren/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ernesto-ren/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ernesto-ren/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null},{"id":81818822,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw4MTgxODgyMg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/57%20-%20Close?","name":"57 - Close?","color":"207de5","default":false,"description":"Issues which may be closable unless discussion continued"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-10-01T13:59:39Z","updated_at":"2024-10-03T14:13:51Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

Turning off Intel's 80-bit extended precision _before_ importing numpy causes the following warning to appear:

```
/home/me/fptest/venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/numpy/_core/getlimits.py:548: UserWarning: Signature b'\x00\xd0\xcc\xcc\xcc\xcc\xcc\xcc\xfb\xbf\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00' for <class 'numpy.longdouble'> does not match any known type: falling back to type probe function.
This warnings indicates broken support for the dtype!
```

To repro we need to build a small C shared library:

```C
// fptest.c
// gcc -Wall -Werror -fpic -shared -o libfptest.so fptest.c

#include <fpu_control.h>

void disable_ext_prec(void) {
  fpu_control_t cw = (_FPU_DEFAULT & ~_FPU_EXTENDED) | _FPU_DOUBLE;
  _FPU_SETCW(cw);
}
```

Drop the .so in the same directory as the python script below and run the script. You should see the warning appear when running on an Intel CPU.

The root cause of the warning seems to be that the binary signature of a long double result when running on Intel with extended precision _disabled_ is not specified in `getlimits.py:_register_known_types`. I think it should be.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
# python3 fptest.py   - run as normal
# python3 fptest.py 1 - run with extended precision disabled

import sys

def disable_ext_prec():
    import ctypes
    libfptest = ctypes.cdll.LoadLibrary("./libfptest.so")
    libfptest.disable_ext_prec()

if len(sys.argv) > 1 and bool(int(sys.argv[1])):
    disable_ext_prec()

import numpy

print(numpy.finfo(numpy.longdouble))

ftype = numpy.longdouble
key = (ftype(-1.0) / ftype(10.))
key = key.view(key.dtype.newbyteorder("<")).tobytes()
print(key)
```


### Error message:

```shell
/home/me/fptest/venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/numpy/_core/getlimits.py:548: UserWarning: Signature b'\x00\xd0\xcc\xcc\xcc\xcc\xcc\xcc\xfb\xbf\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00' for <class 'numpy.longdouble'> does not match any known type: falling back to type probe function.
This warnings indicates broken support for the dtype!
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

```
2.1.1
3.12.7 (main, Oct  1 2024, 08:52:11) [GCC 9.4.0]
```

### Runtime Environment:

```
[{'numpy_version': '2.1.1',
  'python': '3.12.7 (main, Oct  1 2024, 08:52:11) [GCC 9.4.0]',
  'uname': uname_result(system='Linux', node='ubuntu', release='5.15.0-102-generic', version='#112~20.04.1-Ubuntu SMP Thu Mar 14 14:28:24 UTC 2024', machine='x86_64')},
 {'simd_extensions': {'baseline': ['SSE', 'SSE2', 'SSE3'],
                      'found': ['SSSE3',
                                'SSE41',
                                'POPCNT',
                                'SSE42',
                                'AVX',
                                'F16C'],
                      'not_found': ['FMA3',
                                    'AVX2',
                                    'AVX512F',
                                    'AVX512CD',
                                    'AVX512_KNL',
                                    'AVX512_KNM',
                                    'AVX512_SKX',
                                    'AVX512_CLX',
                                    'AVX512_CNL',
                                    'AVX512_ICL']}},
 {'architecture': 'Sandybridge',
  'filepath': '/home/me/fptest/venv/lib/python3.12/site-packages/numpy.libs/libscipy_openblas64_-ff651d7f.so',
  'internal_api': 'openblas',
  'num_threads': 8,
  'prefix': 'libscipy_openblas',
  'threading_layer': 'pthreads',
  'user_api': 'blas',
  'version': '0.3.27'}]
```

This is the real issue.

In my case we don't actually use the long double data type. We perform computations (in doubles) across different architectures which we expect to produce bitwise identical results. When extended precision is enabled we don't get this reproducibility on Intel targets. That's why we turn it off. But then we see the above warning from numpy.

Note that I am not requesting that numpy turn off extended precision on Intel or provide the means to do so. I understand that might not be something that numpy should do. Rather, when extended precision is off the signature of the long double type changes and numpy doesn't recognize it. My request is that this new signature be added to the list of hard coded ones in `getlimits.py`. I think it makes sense to do so since turning off extended precision is a valid thing to do, particularly when looking for reproducibility.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2391533260/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
